coredata.dc:title: DFT study on the sensitivity of silver-graphene quantum dots for vital and harmful analytes

Mahmood, Tariq
Ayub, Khurshid

Sensitivity and selectivity
Gaseous analytes
Electronic properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DOS spectra with HOMO and LUMO visualization of analyte@Ag6-coronene complexes.

e )
calculated at M06-2X/LANL2DZ.
Frontier Molecular Orbitals and UV–Visible analysis.

head.ce:title: DFT study on the sensitivity of silver-graphene quantum dots for vital and harmful analytes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University, Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University, Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan

ce:section.ce:para: The development of smart sensors with the advent of technology in the 21st century much benefited human beings. The emerging advanced sensing devices have been frequently used in environmental monitoring, public health, security, and industries worldwide [1,2]. Various surfaces have been reported for sensing purposes among which graphene got remarkable attention [3–5]. Graphene is 2-D material with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms packed in a honeycomb framework [6]. Graphene exhibits certain unique properties such as high surface area, high electron mobility, high mechanical strength, high thermal and electrical conductivity [7–14]. Due to these properties, graphene attracted the scientific community to explore its applications in optical and electronic devices, energy storage, biotechnology, and sensors [15–18]. The high surface to volume ratio of graphene is perfect for sensing applications. Unfortunately, the pristine graphene is a semi-metal with zero band gap [2]. The surface modification or doping of pristine graphene makes its practical use possible for various applications. Doping can be achieved either by substituting the carbon atom of graphene with an atom having a different number of valence electrons or by the adsorption of dopant over graphene surface [19–26]. Phosphorous doped graphene, due to its excellent properties, has been investigated for energy storage, electrocatalyst, fuel cells, and ammonia sensing [27,28]. Silicon doped graphene has been synthesized and theoretically investigated for its sensing ability for the detection of CO, NO2, H2O, and O2 [29,30].

Among various metals, silver is a potential material for doping due to its excellent electronic properties. It has been reported that the silver decorated graphene hybrid shows magnificent electronic properties. Silver nanoparticles decorated graphene have been experimentally studied for the sensing of various analytes such as metal ions, biological small molecules, nitroaromatics, H2O2, NH3, glucose, O2, H2S, and C2H5OH, etc. [31–33]. Huge amounts of these toxic compounds are released into the environment due to anthropogenic activities. These toxic gases and chemical waste generated from industries, vehicles, etc. show adverse effects on the skin, nervous and respiratory systems of humans. Among different gases, NH3 and H2S are much important due to their toxic and corrosive nature in the environment [34,35]. The concentration of 50–100 ppm of ammonia can cause eye, nose, and throat irritations in most sensitive individuals. Direct exposure to ammonia results in permanent eye damage. Ethanol has been frequently used in narcotics and the emission of carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas, is linked with the processes involved in the production and burning of ethanol [36]. The proper monitoring of oxygen is also essential for the people working in closed spaces. The low supply of oxygen shows detrimental effects for the patients who are convicted with respiratory diseases such as asthma, anemia, etc. [37]. The peoples on earth have a great concern about proper monitoring of these harmful compounds which are badly affecting their life [38–42].

Literature reveals that theoretical investigation about the sensing properties of silver-graphene composites by using a cost-effective DFT method is very limited, especially for NH3, H2S, O2, and C2H5OH has not been reported to date [43–45]. We herein performed a comprehensive comparative study to explore the sensing performance of silver, graphene, and silver-graphene composites. In the current study, we present a coplanar silver cluster over the coronene surface for the selective and accurate determination of these analytes. The current theoretical study hopefully fills the literature gap and will provide a guideline for experimentalists for the development of smart sensors.

Here, we performed a density functional theory (DFT) study to get insight into the adsorption mechanism and noncovalent interactions of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 molecules on the surface of coronene, silver cluster (Ag6), and silver-coronene composites. Quantum chemical calculations are carried out with Gaussian 09, and for structural visualization of results, the Gauss view 5.0 program package is used [46,47]. DFT simulations are performed at the M06-2X/LANL2DZ level of theory [48,49]. M06-2X is a highly parameterized Minnesota functional which is extensively used due to its wide applicability. The LANL2DZ basis set is used for all the atoms (Ag, C, H, S, N, O) in the system. LANL2DZ is an effective core potential (ECP) basis set for transition metal-containing systems.

Vibrational frequencies are calculated to confirm that these optimized structures are true minima (no imaginary frequency. Interaction energies (
E
i n t
) for the adsorption of analytes over coronene, silver cluster and silver-coronene composites are calculated with equations (1)–(3), respectively.
(1)
E int
=
E
coronene … analyte
−
(
E Coronene
+
E analyte
)
(2)
E int
=
E
A
g 6
… analyte
−
(
E
A
g 6
+
E analyte
)
(3)
E int
=
E
(
Coronene − A
g 6
)
… analyte
−
(
E
Coronene − A
g 6
+
E analyte
)

Different orientations of each complex are explored to find their lowest energy structures. NBO charge analysis gives an idea about the amount and direction of charge transferred during interactions. The frontier molecular orbital analysis is used to get an idea about the reactivity of the system. The absorption spectra are calculated with time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) to get a deep understanding of the sensing properties of the system. The density of states (DOS) was conducted by the Gauss sum program to get knowledge about group contribution to molecular orbitals. Non-covalent interactions (NCI) & charge decomposition analysis (CDA) are performed by Multiwfn 3.7 software package to understand the nature of interactions between various fragments of the system [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized geometry and stability of the complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 molecules on the surface of coronene results in interaction energies of −6.81, −4.00, −5.71, and −6.58 kcal mol−1, respectively. The distances between the closest interacting atoms of analyte and substrate in C2H5OH@coronene, NH3@coronene, H2S@coronene, and O2@coronene are 2.57, 3.00, 2.76, and 2.72 Å, respectively. Among studied analytes, ethanol strongly interacts with coronene surface with the least C⋯H interaction distance (Dint). The greater stability of the complex is due to strong interactions between ethanol and coronene. The stability order of the analyte@coronene complexes is as follows; C2H5OH@coronene > O2@coronene > H2S@coronene > NH3@coronene.

The interaction energies for the complexes of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 with the pure silver cluster are −11.64, −16.46, −6.60, and −59.19 kcal mol−1, respectively. The analyte-substrate least interaction distances are O⋯Ag (2.45 Å), N⋯Ag (2.40 Å), H⋯Ag (3.06 Å), and O⋯Ag (2.24 Å) in C2H5OH@Ag6, NH3@Ag6, H2S@Ag6, and O2@Ag6 complexes, respectively. The interactions between analytes and silver cluster in these complexes are electrostatic. The strongest electrostatic interaction is observed between oxygen and silver atoms in the O2@Ag6 complex. The greater electrostatic interaction is due to the high electronegativity of oxygen in the analyte which facilitates the charge transfer from electropositive silver metal to the oxygen atom. The least interaction energy of H2S@Ag6 is because of the electropositive nature of interacting H⋯Ag atoms. The analyte@Ag6 complexes follow the following order of stability i.e. O2@Ag6 > NH3@Ag6 > C2H5OH@Ag6 > H2S@Ag6.

Two configurations of silver-coronene are considered here to investigate their adsorption mechanism i.e. Ag6-coronene and coronene-Ag6. The analyte directly interacts with the silver side in Ag6-coronene whereas with the coronene side in coronene-Ag6 complexes. The interaction energies of C2H5OH@ Ag6-coronene, NH3@Ag6-coronene, H2S@Ag6-coronene, and O2@Ag6-coronene are −10.80, −14.68, −6.47, and −61.06 kcal mol− 1, respectively. The bond distances of closest interacting atoms of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 with Ag6-coronene are O⋯Ag (2.48 Å), N⋯Ag (2.43 Å), S⋯Ag (3.06 Å), and O⋯Ag (2.18 Å), respectively. The analyte@Ag6-coronene complexes show greater stability among all studied complexes which is due to stronger electrostatic interactions between an electronegative atom of analytes with a high electropositive metal atom. The stability order of analyte@Ag6-coronene is as follows; O2@Ag6-coronene > NH3@Ag6-coronene > C2H5OH@Ag6-coronene > H2S@Ag6-coronene. The stability of these analytes over the Ag6-coronene surface is quite similar, as observed in the case of analyte@Ag6 complexes.

The complexes of analytes over coronene-Ag6 configuration show the least interaction energies due to the dominating weak dispersion interactions in them. These complexes are the least stable among all the complexes studied. The interaction energies of C2H5OH@coronene-Ag6, NH3@coronene-Ag6, H2S@coronene-Ag6, and O2@coronene-Ag6 are −5.98, −3.43, −4.83, and −6.26 kcal mol−1, respectively. The least observed distances between analytes and coronene-Ag6 surface for the adsorption of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 are H⋯C (2.69 Å), H⋯C (3.01 Å), H⋯C (2.72 Å), and O⋯C (2.70 Å), respectively. The stability order of analyte@coronene-Ag6 complexes is as follows; O2@coronene-Ag6 > C2H5OH@coronene-Ag6 > H2S@coronene-Ag6 > NH3@coronene-Ag6.

The results of interaction energies depict that pure silver cluster, Ag6-coronene, and coronene-Ag6 surfaces are highly sensitive and selective for oxygen molecule whereas coronene alone is selective for C2H5OH. Ag6-coronene and Ag6 surfaces show the least sensitivity for H2S while coronene and coronene-Ag6 for NH3 molecules.

Natural bond orbital (NBO) charge analysis is performed to get an idea about the amount and direction of charge transfer during complexation. The results of QNBO are displayed in Table 1. The highest amount of charge transfer is observed for the complexes of oxygen which can be seen from the high interaction energies of these complexes (vide supra).

The order of charge transfer is quite consistent with the order of interaction energies for the complexes of oxygen. For the adsorption of ethanol molecule, although the charge transfer trend is like that of charge transfer for oxygen adsorption, the amount of charge transfer is less.

The results of NBO charge analysis reveals that greater charge transfer in analyte@Ag6-coronene and analyte@Ag6 complexes is due to strong electrostatic interactions between highly electropositive silver and highly electronegative atom of analytes. The less amount of charge transfer in analyte@coronene-Ag6 and analyte@coronene complexes might be due to weaker van der Waals dispersion interactions in them.

The reactivity of the system is investigated through the changes in energies of occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) upon interactions with analytes. The HOMO is the electron donor while LUMO is the electron acceptor. The increase in conductivity is a consequence of the reduction in the HOMO-LUMO gap and vice versa.

The HOMO and LUMO orbitals of coronene are present at −6.79 and −0.90 eV, respectively with an H-Lgap of 5.89 eV. The complexation of coronene with C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 molecules results in H-L gaps of 5.87, 5.89, 5.88, and 4.07 eV, respectively.

The reduction in H-Lgap is mainly due to a decrease in the energy of virtual orbitals on complexation. The percentages of reduction in H-Lgap in C2H5OH@coronene, NH3@coronene, H2S@coronene, and O2@coronene complexes compared to coronene alone are 0.3, 0, 0.1, and 30.8%, respectively. A prominent decrease in H-Lgap is observed in the O2@coronene complex which is probably due to greater charge transfer interaction in the complex. Although, the energy of occupied and virtual orbitals change but to the same extent so there is no net change in H-Lgap on interactions of ammonia with coronene. The poor orbitals overlap in NH3@coronene can be realized from low interaction energy and less charge transfer in the complex.

In a pure silver cluster, the EHOMO and ELUMO are −5.76 and −1.74 eV, respectively with an observed H-Lgap of 4.02 eV. On the interaction of silver cluster with C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 molecules, the H-L gaps are reduced to 3.94, 3.91, 3.99, and 3.73 eV, respectively. A greater decrease is noticed in the O2@Ag6 complex which might be due to stronger Ag⋯O electrostatic interactions. The percent reduction in H-Lgap on interaction with analytes compared to a pure silver cluster is O2@Ag6 (1.9%) > NH3@Ag6 (2.7%) > C2H5OH@Ag6 (0.7%) > H2S@Ag6 (7.2%). The order is the same as we observed in the case of interaction energy for these complexes. The reduction in H-Lgap is a result of stronger interactions in these complexes.

The HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the silver-coronene composite are located at −5.17 and −1.34 eV, respectively. The H-Lgap in a silver-coronene composite is 3.83 eV which is lower than either coronene or silver cluster alone. The interaction of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 over the silver side of the silver-coronene composite results in a decrease in H-Lgap to 3.77, 3.52, 3.82, and 3.70 eV, respectively. The order of percent reduction in H-Lgap in these complexes are NH3@Ag6-coronene (8.09%) > O2@Ag6-coronene (3.39%) > C2H5OH@Ag6-coronene (1.5%) > H2S@Ag6-coronene (0.2%). The highest decrease in H-Lgap is noticed in NH3 while the least effect is seen in H2S over Ag6-coronene composite. In the case of adsorption of C2H5OH, NH3, H2S, and O2 over the coronene side of silver-coronene composite, the H-L gaps of 3.84, 3.83, 3.84, and 2.20 eV, respectively are observed. The percent decrease in H-Lgap follow the following order for these complexes i.e. O2@coronene-Ag6 (42.5%) > NH3@coronene-Ag6 (0%) > C2H5OH@coronene-Ag6 (−0.2%) > H2S@ coronene-Ag6 (−0.2%). The greater conductivity of O2@coronene-Ag6 is attributed to the prominent decrease in H-Lgap in the complex. Like NH3@coronene, H-Lgap is not changed in the NH3@coronene-Ag6 complex. In the case of adsorption of C2H5OH and H2S over coronene-Ag6, the H-L gaps somewhat increase which results in a slight decrease in conductivity of these complexes.

The DOS spectra of the complexes advocate that, on the interaction of analytes with coronene surface, there are various energy states generated in both occupied and virtual sides. For the complexes of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2 molecules with coronene the new energy states appeared in virtual orbitals at −1.09, −1.08, −1.00, and −2.81 eV, respectively. The generation of new energy states in LUMO orbitals at lower energy validate that, on complexation, the conductivity of complexes increases. In complexes of all analytes with coronene, the HOMO and LUMO are present at the coronene surface except for oxygen where LUMO is located over the analyte. The LUMO is an electron acceptor and its presence over the analyte in the O2@coronene complex indicates that the electronic cloud is transferred towards the analyte.

The interaction of analytes with pure silver cluster results in the generation of new energy states near to the Fermi level in DOS spectra. New energy states are created in virtual orbital at −1.59, −1.70, −1.24 and −2.12 eV for the complexes of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2, respectively, with a silver cluster. The generation of energy states of lower energy in the LUMO side of spectra signifies the reduction in H-Lgap and increase in conductivity upon complexation. In the O2@Ag6 complex, the HOMO and LUMO are partially located over the analyte and silver cluster whereas, for other analytes@Ag6 complexes, HOMO and LUMO are present at the silver cluster. The occurrence of LUMO over analyte on O2@Ag6 affirms that the electronic cloud is shifted towards analyte in the complex.

The DOS spectra for the complexes of analytes with silver-coronene composites reveals that new energy states appeared in occupied and virtual orbitals closer to the Fermi level. The appearance of the greater number of energy states in both HOMO and LUMO sides indicates that the conductivity of silver-coronene composites is greatly enhanced on interaction with analytes. The orbital hybridization results in a significant reduction of the energy gap between occupied and virtual orbitals. The interaction of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2 with Ag6-coronene results in the generation of new energy states in the LUMO side at −1.22, −1.44, −1.30, and −1.44 eV, respectively. The variation of energy states reflects the greater conductivity on complexation. The HOMO and LUMO orbitals are located on a silver cluster in C2H5OH@Ag6-coronene, H2S@Ag6-coronene, and NH3@Ag6-coronene complexes. In O2@Ag6-coronene, the HOMO is also spread over the silver cluster but LUMO over the coronene surface.

For another configuration of silver-coronene (i.e. analyte@coronene-Ag6), new virtual orbitals are found at −1.43, −1.42, −1.39, and −3.02 eV for the adsorption of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2, respectively. The LUMO in O2@coronene-Ag6 is greatly shifted towards lower energy that ultimately causes a significant reduction in H-Lgap and increases the conductivity of the complex (vide supra). The HOMO and LUMO orbitals are located over the silver cluster in all complexes except for O2@coronene-Ag6 where LUMO is present over analyte (oxygen molecule). The high electronegativity of the oxygen atom of the analyte enables the shifting of the electronic cloud towards itself. Moreover, the generation of a greater number of energy states in virtual orbitals compared to the occupied orbital indorse the better sensing ability of silver-coronene composites towards analytes.

The bare coronene shows maximum absorbance at 262 nm where excitation energy of 4.71 eV is needed for crucial π→π* transition from HOMO−1→LUMO. The complexation of coronene with C2H5OH and H2S results in slight red shifting of
λ max
to 264 nm. The variation in maximum absorbance of coronene on interaction is 0.7% which illustrates its insignificant response for these analytes. The energy required for HOMO−1→LUMO+1 transition in the C2H5OH@coronene complex is 4.69 eV along with an oscillator strength of 0.98 a.u. The energy required for the transition from HOMO→LUMO+1 in the H2S@coronene complex is 4.69 eV beside oscillator strength of 0.97 a.u. In the complexes of NH3 and O2 with coronene, there is no variation found in absorbance maximum (
λ max
= 262 nm). The 0% shifting of
λ max
in NH3@coronene, is quite consistent with its 0% reduction in H-Lgap. The dominant transitions in NH3@coronene and O2@coronene are from HOMO−1→LUMO+1 and HOMO−1→LUMO+2, respectively.

The
λ max
for silver cluster is noticed at 394 nm and energy of 3.14 eV is associated with HOMO →LUMO+2 transition. For C2H5OH@Ag6, O2@Ag6, and NH3@Ag6 the
λ max
is shifted towards a longer wavelength of 405, 404, and 396 nm, respectively. In the H2S@Ag6 complex, the
λ max
is shifted towards a shorter wavelength (hypsochromic) at 393 nm. These systems can be used for chemical sensing applications with visual changes. Generally, the
λ max
is shifted towards a longer wavelength upon complexation due to a decrease in H-Lgap. The larger bathochromic shift is observed for NH3@Ag6 (2.7%) while least for C2H5OH@Ag6 (0.5%). The crucial transitions along with excitation energies for the adsorption of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2 over silver cluster are from HOMO→LUMO+3 (3.12 eV), HOMO→LUMO+3 (3.15 eV), HOMO−1→LUMO (3.06 eV), and HOMO →LUMO+2 (3.06 eV), respectively. The larger bathochromic shift in the complexes of analytes with silver cluster expresses that the conductivity of these complexes increases on interactions.

The H-L gap in the silver-coronene composite is 3.83 eV besides the excitation energy of 2.84 eV, which is required for HOMO−1→LUMO+4 transition. Analytes are separately adsorbed over silver and coronene sides of the composites. For the adsorption over the silver side, the maximum absorbance of silver-coronene composite changes from 435 nm to 438, 434, 434, and 460 nm due to adsorption of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2, respectively. The larger red shift (5.7%) is noticed in O2@Ag6-coronene which results from a substantial reduction in the energy gap between occupied and virtual orbitals. The important transitions for the complexes of C2H5OH and NH3 with silver-coronene are from HOMO→LUMO+4 beside excitation energies of 2.82 and 2.85 eV, respectively. Slight hypsochromic shifts are seen in H2S@Ag6-coronene and NH3@Ag6-coronene complexes. Interaction of analytes with Ag6-coronene cause substantial variation in its conductivity by changing its H-Lgap.

The adsorption of C2H5OH, H2S, NH3, and O2 over the coronene side of the composite results in a change in
λ max
to 432, 433, 434, and 483 nm, respectively. The hypsochromic shift in
λ max
is detected for O2@coronene-Ag6, whereas bathochromic shifts are observed in C2H5OH@coronene-Ag6, H2S@coronene-Ag6, and NH3@coronene-Ag6. The greater red shift (11%) is found in O2@coronene-Ag6 which is quite consistent with a larger reduction in its H-Lgap. The energy of 2.20 eV is associated with important HOMO−3→LUMO transition in the O2@coronene-Ag6 complex. The substantial red shift of
λ max
besides the decrease in excitation energy, validate the greater sensitivity of silver-coronene composites towards oxygen molecule. The visual changes in spectra demonstrate that silver-coronene can be used as a chemical sensor for these analytes.

NCI‐RDG 2D scatter map and 3D color‐filled RDG isosurface for the complexes of analytes with Ag6-coronene are given in Fig. 7 (For the rest of the complexes see supporting information). NCI is used here to get a clear idea about various types of interactions involved in the complexes such as electrostatic, van der Waals, and steric repulsions. Non-covalent interactions are distinguished by color codes; blue and green colors indicate electrostatic and van der Waals interactions, respectively while red color represents repulsions in the complex. NCI graph of analyte@coronene complexes shows that van der Waals interaction and steric clashes are present in these complexes. The low-density gradient peaks at −0.01 a.u in the RDG map indicate van der Waals (H⋯C) dispersion interactions which can be seen from the green disc between analytes and coronene isosurface. Red peak associated with steric repulsions appears at 0.02 a.u for C2H5OH@coronene, H2S@coronene, and NH3@coronene whereas, for O2@coronene complex, it appears at 0.01 to 0.02 a.u.

The non-covalent interactions in analytes@Ag6 are strong electrostatic interactions and steric repulsions. The peaks related to electrostatic interactions appear at −0.03 to −0.02 a.u. while, for repulsive forces, they appear at 0.02 a.u. The electrostatic forces have a strong influence on the stability which can be depicted from the higher interaction energies of these complexes. The dominant electrostatic (O⋯Ag) interactions are responsible for the higher interaction energy of the O2@Ag6 complex. Moreover, repulsions dominate in the H2S@Ag6 complex which results in its lower stability. The peak associated with van der Waals interaction is completely absent in analyte@Ag6 complexes.

NCI results reveal that three types of non-covalent interactions are present in the complexes of analytes with silver-coronene. The controlling forces in analyte@Ag6-coronene and analyte@coronene-Ag6 complexes are electrostatic and van der Waals dispersion interactions, respectively. Although the position of peaks is similar, but their intensities are different in both configurations. The low-density peaks associated with the electrostatic interactions originate at −0.03 to −0.02 a.u. in all complexes except for O2@Ag6-coronene where peaks appear at −0.04 to −0.02 a.u. The wide range of electrostatic forces affirms the higher interaction between oxygen molecule and silver-coronene in the O2@Ag6-coronene complex. The peaks appearing at −0.01 and 0.02 a.u. correspond to van der Waals dispersion interactions and steric repulsion in the complexes, respectively.
CDA is performed to estimate the relative strength of charge transfer via donation, backdonation, and repulsion between various fragment pairs of the complex.

The repulsive polarization of charges indicates that charge density accumulates in the binding region in NH3–Ag6 and NH3-coronene fragments pairs of NH3@Ag6 and NH3@coronene complexes. For various fragment pairs of the complexes of C2H5OH, H2S, and O2 with silver cluster and coronene alone, the charge density moves away from the interaction region.

The values of repulsive polarization for analytes@Ag6-coronene suggest that charge density moves aside in various fragment pairs except in H2S@Ag6-coronene (H2S-coronene), NH3@Ag6-coronene (NH3–Ag6 NH3-coronene), and O2@Ag6-coronene (O2–Ag6, O2-coronene & Ag6-coronene) complexes. In another configuration i.e. analytes@coronene-Ag6, the accumulation of charge densities is observed in C2H5OH@coronene-Ag6 (C2H5OH–Ag6), H2S@coronene-Ag6 (H2S–Ag6), NH3@coronene-Ag6 (NH3–Ag6, NH3-coronene), and O2@coronene-Ag6 (O2–Ag6) complexes.

For the rest of the fragments pairs of other complexes, the repulsion of charges indicates that charge densities move away during interaction from the binding region. Generally, charge transfer through backdonation dominates over the donation, and moving away of charge density from interaction region is noticed between various fragment pairs of the complexes. Moreover, a greater charge transfer is observed between the silver cluster and analyte fragments pair of the complexes.
Tabish Jadoon: Conceptualization, Methodology, Calculations, Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation.
Tariq Mahmood: Visualization, Investigation, Original draft preparation.
Khurshid Ayub: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Original draft preparation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial and technical support from the COMSATS University Islamabad and Higher Education Commission of Pakistan (Grant # 1899, 2469, 2981).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1
Table S1–S15 shows the interaction energies. Table S16 represents CDA. Fig. SS1 shows DOS spectra with HOMO-LUMO visualization. Fig. SS2 shows the NCI-2D RDG map with isosurface.
Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2021.110028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in smart environment monitoring systems using IoT and sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.L. Ullo, G.R. Sinha, Advances in Smart Environment Monitoring Systems Using IoT and Sensors, Sensors. 20 (2020) 3113. doi:10.3390/s20113113.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disposable sensors in diagnostics, food, and environmental monitoring
